trigger_situations: Confirming plans, ending discussions, accepting offers, showing readiness, giving approval
contexts: Texting, casual conversations, phone calls, informal meetings, everyday interactions
variations: c'est bon, c'est ok, c'est négociable, ça marche, ça roule
common_mistakes: Overusing instead of specific responses; confusing tone (can sound dismissive); using in formal contexts where 'c'est d'accord' is appropriate
collocates: c'est bon, ouais c'est bon, c'est bon ça, bon c'est bon
example_2: Tu peux dire c'est bon quand tu veux exprimer it's fine / okay / that's good.
example_3: On utilise c'est bon dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say it's good when you want to express it's fine / okay / that's good.
example_3_translation: We use it well in daily life.

communicative_purpose: Asking for definition, explanation, or identification
trigger_situations: Asking about objects, concepts, situations, unclear references, definitions
contexts: Casual conversations, text messages, learning contexts, informal questioning, everyday dialogue
variations: c'est quoi, c'est ça, c'est que, c'est lequel, c'est qui
common_mistakes: Intonation issues (sounds like statement); forgetting 'ci' for 'this' vs 'ça' for 'that'; awkward with nouns that need 'un/une'
collocates: c'est quoi, c'est quoi ça, c'est quoi ce, c'est quoi exactement
example_2: Tu peux dire c'est quoi quand tu veux exprimer what is it / what's that.
example_3: On utilise c'est quoi dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say what's what when you want to express what is it / what's that.
example_3_translation: What do we use in daily life?

communicative_purpose: Asking about someone's present action or whereabouts
trigger_situations: Checking in, texting, social moments, making plans, casual check-ups
contexts: Texting, phone calls, social media, casual meetings, everyday conversations
variations: tu fais quoi, tu fabriques quoi, tu concocts quoi, tu trafiques quoi
common_mistakes: Overly direct for formal situations; sounding controlling; confusing with 'tu vas faire quoi' (future); pronominal verb needs 'te'
collocates: tu fais quoi, tu fais quoi aujourd'hui, tu fais quoi de beau, tu fous quoi (vulgaire)
example_2: Tu peux dire tu fais quoi quand tu veux exprimer what are you doing.
example_3: On utilise tu fais quoi dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say what you do when you want to express what are you doing.
example_3_translation: We use what do you do in daily life.

communicative_purpose: Expressing lack of knowledge, indifference, or uncertainty
trigger_situations: Not knowing answer, not caring, avoiding decision, passive resistance, genuine uncertainty
contexts: Casual conversations, text messages, discussions, everyday interactions, informal settings
variations: je sais pas, j'en ai aucune idée, aucune idée, j'sais pas du tout, pas la moindre idée
common_mistakes: Dropping 'ne' in writing (formal still requires it); overuse as conversation filler; sounding dismissive when not intended
collocates: je sais pas, j'sais pas, je sais pas trop, j'en sais rien, je sais pas encore
example_2: Tu peux dire je sais pas quand tu veux exprimer I don't know.
example_3: On utilise je sais pas dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say I don't know when you want to express I don't know.
example_3_translation: We use I don't know in daily life.

communicative_purpose: Expressing shock, surprise, concern, or being overwhelmed
trigger_situations: Receiving bad news, facing unexpected situations, being confused, reacting to surprises
contexts: Casual conversations, text messages, reactions to news, expressive moments, informal dialogue
variations: oula, oh la la, oh là, oulala, beh ouf
common_mistakes: Can sound dated depending on region; overusing diminishes impact; confusing register (too casual for some situations)
collocates: oula, oula oui, oula non, oula là, ben oula
example_1: J'utilise souvent oula pour parler de whoa / wow / oh boy.
example_2: Tu peux dire oula quand tu veux exprimer whoa / wow / oh boy.
example_3: On utilise oula dans la vie quotidienne.
example_1_translation: I often use oula to talk about whoa/wow/oh boy.
example_2_translation: You can say oula when you want to express whoa/wow/oh boy.
example_3_translation: We use oula in daily life.

communicative_purpose: Summarizing, transitioning, dismissing side topics, concluding
trigger_situations: Ending explanations, getting to the point, dismissing details, concluding stories
contexts: Casual conversations, storytelling, texting, everyday speech, informal discussions
variations: bref, en résumé, en gros, voila, sinon
common_mistakes: Overusing as filler; using in formal writing; can sound dismissive or impatient; context needed for tone
collocates: bref, et bref, mais bref, donc bref, quoi bref
example_1: J'utilise souvent bref pour parler de in short / anyway / basically.
example_2: Tu peux dire bref quand tu veux exprimer in short / anyway / basically.
example_3: On utilise bref dans la vie quotidienne.
example_1_translation: I often use brief to talk about in short / anyway / basically.
example_2_translation: You can say brief when you want to express in short / anyway / basically.
example_3_translation: We use brief in everyday life.

communicative_purpose: Connecting causes to consequences, explaining results, casual reasoning
trigger_situations: Explaining consequences, making logical connections, casual argumentation, cause-effect
contexts: Everyday speech, texting, casual conversations, informal discussions, storytelling
variations: du coup, du coup, alors, donc, du coup ça fait que
common_mistakes: Overusing as filler (very common in youth speech); formal contexts require 'donc' or 'par conséquent'; can obscure actual reasoning
collocates: du coup, et du coup, du coup on, ben du coup, du coup c'est
example_2: Tu peux dire du coup quand tu veux exprimer so / therefore / as a result.
example_3: On utilise du coup dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say when you want to express so / therefore / as a result.
example_3_translation: We therefore use it in daily life.

communicative_purpose: Agreeing, confirming obvious points, casual yes responses
trigger_situations: Simple agreement, confirming obvious things, casual conversations, text responses
contexts: Texting, phone calls, informal chats, everyday interactions, casual confirmations
variations: bah ouais, ouais, ouais ouais, ouais ouais ouais, ouais mais
common_mistakes: Sounds very casual/regional; inappropriate for formal situations; varying pronunciation of 'bah' vs 'ben'
collocates: bah ouais, bah ouais clairement, bah ouais carrément, bah ouais bien sûr
example_2: Tu peux dire bah ouais quand tu veux exprimer yeah / yep / well yeah.
example_3: On utilise bah ouais dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say bah yeah when you want to express yeah / yep / well yeah.
example_3_translation: We use it, yeah, in everyday life.

meaning: it's crazy / it's insane / it's mad
communicative_purpose: Describing intense situations, chaos, excitement, or madness
trigger_situations: Describing crazy events, expressing overwhelm, reacting to chaos, describing兴奋ement
contexts: Casual conversations, texting about events, social situations, expressing reactions, informal speech
variations: c'est la folie, c'est dingue, c'est fou, c'est n'importe quoi, c'est le chaos
common_mistakes: Can sound hyperbolic; regional variations; intensity may not translate; overuse diminishes impact
collocates: c'est la folie, c'est trop la folie, c'est un peu la folie, c'est grave la folie
example_2: Tu peux dire c'est la folie quand tu veux exprimer it's crazy / it's insane / it's mad.
example_3: On utilise c'est la folie dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say it's madness when you want to express it's crazy / it's insane / it's mad.
example_3_translation: We use it's madness in everyday life.

meaning: we'll see / it remains to be seen
communicative_purpose: Expressing uncertainty about outcomes, conditional decisions, pending situations
trigger_situations: Uncertain plans, pending decisions, future considerations, conditional commitments
contexts: Casual planning, discussing possibilities, tentative agreements, everyday decision-making
variations: faut voir, on verra bien, ça reste à voir, on en reparle, wait and see
common_mistakes: Can sound evasive if overused; different from 'peut-être'; tone can seem noncommittal; formal situations need more clarity
collocates: faut voir, on verra, faut voir venir, faut voir encore, peut-être faut voir
example_2: Tu peux dire faut voir quand tu veux exprimer we'll see / it remains to be seen.
example_3: On utilise faut voir dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say must see when you want to express we'll see / it remains to be seen.
example_3_translation: We use it in everyday life.

communicative_purpose: Emphasizing honesty, expressing frustration, adding weight to statements
trigger_situations: Expressing opinions strongly, adding emphasis, complaining, honest assessments, frustrated reactions
contexts: Casual conversations, texting, informal discussions, expressing frustration, everyday speech
variations: franchement, sincèrement, honnêtement,series seriously, sans dec
common_mistakes: Can sound preachy if overused; tone matters ('franchement' as question vs statement); register issues in very formal contexts
collocates: franchement, Franchement? (surprised), Franchement speaking, Franchement non, Franchement oui
example_2: Tu peux dire franchement quand tu veux exprimer frankly / honestly / seriously.
example_3: On utilise franchement dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say frankly when you want to express frankly / honestly / seriously.
example_3_translation: We use it frankly in daily life.

trigger_situations: Confirming plans, expressing certainty, agreeing strongly, making promises
contexts: Casual conversations, texting, making plans, expressing conviction, informal agreements
variations: c'est sûr, c'est certain, c'est clair, c'est sûr et certain, c'est garanti
common_mistakes: Confusing with 'c'est certain' (more formal); can sound overly confident; needs context to avoid sounding presumptuous
collocates: c'est sûr, ouais c'est sûr, c'est sûr ouais, c'est hyper sûr, c'est certain
example_2: Tu peux dire c'est sûr quand tu veux exprimer for sure / definitely.
example_3: On utilise c'est sûr dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say it's sure when you want to express for sure/definitely.
example_3_translation: We use it for sure in daily life.

meaning: it's serious / that's bad / it's intense
communicative_purpose: Describing seriousness, expressing concern, indicating intensity
trigger_situations: Reacting to problems, describing severity, expressing concern, reacting to news
contexts: Casual conversations, texting, reactions to news, everyday discussions, informal speech
variations: c'est grave, c'est sérieux, c'est balèze, c'est chaud, c'est pas jojo
common_mistakes: 'C'est pas grave' means 'it's fine/don't worry' (opposite of concern); context crucial; regional expressions vary
collocates: c'est grave, c'est pas grave, c'est trop grave, c'est un peu grave, c'est grave ou pas
example_2: Tu peux dire c'est grave quand tu veux exprimer it's serious / that's bad / it's intense.
example_3: On utilise c'est grave dans la vie quotidienne.
example_2_translation: You can say it's serious when you want to express it's serious / that's bad / it's intense.
example_3_translation: We use it seriously in daily life.

meaning: meh / not great / I don't know
communicative_purpose: Expressing lukewarm feelings, mild disagreement, or indifference
trigger_situations: Responding to offers, expressing mild disinterest, lukewarm responses, casual dismissals
contexts: Casual conversations, texting, informal responses, everyday interactions, casual rejections
variations: bof, moyen, comme ça, pas fou, ni chaud ni froid
common_mistakes: Can sound dismissive or rude depending on context; tone matters greatly; not suitable for formal situations; may sound dated in some regions
collocates: bof, bof bof, bof bof bof, bof un peu, bof j'sais pas
example_1: J'utilise souvent bof pour parler de meh / not great / I don't know.
example_2: Tu peux dire bof quand tu veux exprimer meh / not great / I don't know.
example_3: On utilise bof dans la vie quotidienne.
example_1_translation: I often use bof to talk about meh / not great / I don't know.
example_2_translation: You can say blah when you want to express meh / not great / I don't know.
example_3_translation: We use bof in everyday life.

communicative_purpose: Giving examples, expressing disbelief, adding emphasis, casual storytelling
trigger_situations: Giving examples, expressing surprise, storytelling, adding emphasis, casual conversation
contexts: Casual conversations, texting, everyday speech, informal storytelling, youth language
variations: genre, comme, style, genre genre, like
common_mistakes: Overusing as filler (very common in youth speech); can sound vague; overusing diminishes meaning; register very informal
collocates: genre, genre ouais, genre non, c'est genre, genre comme, genre quoi
example_1: J'utilise souvent genre pour parler de like / for example / like seriously.
example_2: Tu peux dire genre quand tu veux exprimer like / for example / like seriously.
example_3: On utilise genre dans la vie quotidienne.
example_1_translation: I often use genre to talk about like / for example / like seriously.
example_2_translation: You can say like when you want to express like / for example / like seriously.
